planting.note: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost, or plant container stock in spring in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, alkaline to neutral, lean, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; Thymbra spicata is native to rocky limestone slopes in Turkey, Cyprus, and the Levant and demands excellent drainage and low soil fertility.

directions: Plant in well-drained, dry, lean, alkaline to neutral, sandy or stony soil in full sun; never plant in heavy, wet, or fertile soil, which causes rot and premature death. Thymbra spicata (zahter) is a small, woody, drought-tolerant perennial herb native to rocky limestone hillsides in Turkey, Cyprus, Syria, Lebanon, and Israel — a close relative of thyme (Thymus) with a similar but distinctly more savory, spicy-bitter, oregano-like aroma and flavor profile. It is one of the traditional species used to make the Middle Eastern spice blend 'za'atar' (which includes multiple wild herb species depending on region), and the fresh or dried leaves and flowering tops are used to season meats, breads, cheese, and olive oil throughout Turkey and the Levant. In Turkish cuisine, zahter (za'atar in Arabic/Hebrew) is a primary herb for the spice blend used with olive oil and flatbread. The plant produces dense, spike-like inflorescences of pink to purple flowers in summer that are excellent for pollinators. In zone 9, it thrives in dry, alkaline, well-drained conditions and is highly drought-resistant once established.
sources: Purdue University — New Crop Resources — Thymbra spicata
Herb Society of America — Za'atar Herbs

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost and transplant after frost danger; tomatillos are self-infertile — plant at least two for cross-pollination and fruit set; in zone 9, start seeds in January–February for March–April transplanting, or direct sow in March for summer harvest.

directions: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting; set transplants in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; plant at least two for pollination. Physalis philadelphica (tomatillo) is the green or purple, husked fruit essential to Mexican salsas, mole verde, and enchilada sauce — a fruity, tart, citrusy tomato relative encased in a papery, lantern-like calyx that expands as the fruit develops and splits open to signal ripeness; harvest when the husk fills tightly and the fruit turns from green toward yellow, or use green for maximum tartness in salsa verde. Tomatillos are extremely productive warm-season annuals that bear heavily in zone 9 through summer and fall; they are more heat-tolerant than tomatoes and continue producing when summer temperatures exceed 90°F. The plants sprawl considerably and benefit from caging or staking; prune to 2–3 main stems for higher yields. 'Verde Puebla' and 'Gigante' are large-fruited green types; 'Purple de Milpa' has purple-tinged fruits. Unlike tomatoes, tomatillos must be planted in pairs or groups because they are self-incompatible — single plants produce no fruit.

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container stock in late winter to early spring in moist to wet, acidic, humus-rich, occasionally flooded soil in full sun to part shade; mayhaw is native to bottomlands, pond margins, and stream banks in the Gulf South and requires moist to seasonally wet acidic soils for best production.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, acidic, humus-rich, well-drained to occasionally flooded soil in full sun; mayhaw thrives in the heavy, wet, acidic soils of the Gulf Coast plain that frustrate most fruit trees. Crataegus aestivalis (mayhaw) is the iconic native fruit tree of the Deep South and Gulf Coast, producing small (1/2-to-3/4-inch), bright-red to orange-red, apple-like fruits in April–May (hence 'may haw' — haw is an old name for hawthorn fruit) that are traditionally harvested by spreading sheets under the tree or raking from the water's surface when the tree grows over ponds and bayous. Mayhaw jelly is a Southern culinary tradition — the vivid, translucent, coral-red jelly with a unique tart-sweet, apple-berry flavor is considered a regional treasure and a gift-worthy specialty product throughout Louisiana, Texas, and Mississippi. The tree is also highly ornamental — producing masses of white flowers in February–March (often among the first spring blossoms), followed by the distinctive April berry crop, then good fall foliage color. Named cultivars for improved fruit production include 'Big Red,' 'Crimson,' 'Super Berry,' and 'Texas Star.' Mayhaw grows naturally in wet bottomland forests, bayou margins, and pond edges throughout the Gulf Coastal Plain.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Native Fruits

planting.note: Plant bare-root stock in early spring before bud break in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to alkaline, moderately fertile soil in full sun; American plum is one of the most adaptable native fruit trees, thriving from the Great Plains to the Atlantic Coast in well-drained to dry soils.

directions: Plant bare-root trees in early spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, moderately fertile soil in full sun; American plum is drought-tolerant once established and naturalizes readily in hedgerows and woodland edges. Prunus americana (American wild plum) is a native small tree or large shrub found across much of the eastern and central United States, producing clusters of fragrant white flowers in March–April before leaf emergence (a spectacular early-spring display that attracts native bees, especially mining bees and andrenid bees) followed by abundant 3/4-inch, red to orange-yellow plums in July–August. The fruits are quite tart when fully ripe but develop excellent plum flavor when fully ripe and dead-ripe, and are highly prized for jams, jellies, wines, and preserves; they were a staple food of many Native American peoples and European settlers. American plum spreads by root suckers to form thickets valuable for wildlife (providing dense nesting cover for songbirds and food for over 70 bird species), and has been extensively used as rootstock for grafting hybrid plum cultivars. It is among the most cold-hardy, drought-tolerant, and disease-resistant of all plums.
sources: University of Minnesota Extension — Native Fruit Plants

planting.note: Plant aerial bulbils or underground tubers after last frost in well-drained, sandy to loamy, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; air potato is an extremely vigorous tropical vine that can grow 20–30 feet in a single season — site with care to avoid the plant overwhelming support structures or neighboring plants. Note: Dioscorea bulbifera is a USDA Federal Noxious Weed listed as invasive in Florida — check local regulations before planting.

directions: Plant aerial bulbils (the small, potato-like structures that form along the stems) or underground tubers after last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile soil with a sturdy trellis, fence, or tree for support; air potato grows extremely rapidly in warm conditions. IMPORTANT: In Florida and other warm states, Dioscorea bulbifera is listed as invasive and its cultivation is regulated or prohibited — consult USDA and state Department of Agriculture guidelines before planting. Dioscorea bulbifera is a tropical Asian vine grown widely in Africa, South and Southeast Asia, and the Pacific Islands as a minor food crop — the aerial bulbils (small, edible tubers that develop at the leaf axils) and the underground tubers are both edible after cooking, though many wild or feral populations contain potentially toxic compounds that require thorough boiling in multiple changes of water to render safe; cultivated varieties are generally milder. In markets across West Africa and Asia, edible cultivars are distinguished from bitter, toxic ones by appearance and flavor; seek out named edible cultivars from specialty tropical plant sources. The vine is a remarkably productive food plant in tropical gardens but must be managed aggressively to prevent escape.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Invasive Plants
